annotations.text: Because the finding of proctitis is always abnormal and indicates an underlying disease process, I will develop my differential diagnosis around entities that could cause proctitis in the context of this patient’s perianal findings and epidemiologic risk factors.
the presence of other anorectal abnormalities in this patient raises doubts about whether he really had hemorrhoids.
If we rule out all these entities from our differential diagnosis, we are left with Crohn’s disease and a number of infectious diseases.

The description of this patient’s proctitis is not consistent with ulceration, and therefore, we can rule out several common causes of ulcerative proctitis, including herpes simplex virus–associated proctitis, chancroid, and granuloma inguinale caused by the bacteria  *Klebsiella granulomatis*
the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
On the basis of the patient’s sexual history, syphilis is more likely than inflammatory bowel disease.
The onset of secondary syphilis typically occurs 4 to 12 weeks after exposure; although symptoms typically resolve in 3 to 12 weeks, they may last for months.
Overall, the features and timeline in this case are consistent with syphilis.
Rash is a nearly universal feature of secondary syphilis, affecting 93% of MSM with secondary syphilis, so its absence should give us pause.

lymphogranuloma venereum causes an isolated proctitis without lymphadenitis in 83% of cases that are acquired by anogenital contact, and the secondary stage can mimic Crohn’s disease, with features including perianal fissures, fistulae, and abscesses.
In a review of cases in which MSM presented to a surgery clinic for treatment of proctitis, syphilis was a more common cause than Crohn’s disease.
Between January 2013 and January 2014, the number of reported cases of syphilis increased by 15%, and in 2014, a total of 83% of men with primary and secondary syphilis were in the MSM population.
When I saw this patient, he had multiple large fissures or ulcers, which prompted me to perform the sigmoidoscopy, as I would do in any patient with new anorectal findings.
